title: From Medical School Dreams to the Reality of Healthcare in Iran
description: A personal story about education, the Konkur exam, financial pressure, and working inside the Iranian healthcare system.
author: Amin

blocks.content:
## Introduction

Almost everyone around me believed that education was the only safe path toward becoming someone valuable.

And honestly, I believed it too.

I wanted to become skilled at something meaningful.
I wanted a future I could be proud of.

But the education system I grew up in was deeply broken.

...

***

## The Iranian Education System and the Konkur Exam

The problem was the system itself.

It wasn’t designed to help people think bigger.
It wasn’t designed to help students understand why they were learning something or what they could eventually build with it.

It felt more like a system designed to keep your vision small.

...

### Why the Konkur Exam Feels Like Survival

For millions of students, that exam represents survival.

Not just success.

Survival.

Because the economy is weak, private business opportunities are limited, and many families depend on government jobs for stability.

...

### Educational Inequality and University Quotas

But even that competition never felt completely fair.

There are government-supported quotas called “Sahmieh,” where certain groups receive major advantages in university admissions because of family ties connected to war history.

...

***

## Studying for Medical School During Family and Financial Crisis

Still, I studied.

For three years, my entire life revolved around that exam.

Then during my final year, my family lost almost everything because of a financial scam.

...

This was the first real competition for my future that I had ever experienced.

And I took it seriously.

***

## The Conflict Between Education and Real Learning

The deeper I went into that system, the more conflicted I became.

Because on one side, effort absolutely mattered.

...

### Why I Questioned the Education System

I used to ask myself questions like:

“If someone wants to become a doctor, why not push them toward reading medical research, scientific articles, or real case studies?”

Why not encourage curiosity?

Instead, we memorized things simply because they might appear as multiple-choice questions on an exam.

...

***

## Getting Accepted Into Anesthesia Technology

Still, after years of pressure and studying, I finally passed the exam and got accepted into Anesthesia Technology.

And considering the conditions around me, that counted as success.

At least, that’s what I thought.

***

## The Reality of Working Inside Hospitals

Everything changed when I entered the hospital.

That was the moment reality hit me.

For years, people talked about medical careers as if they guaranteed stability, respect, and security.

But inside the hospitals, I saw exhausted workers barely surviving financially.

...

### Inflation, Low Salaries, and Healthcare Workers

There had already been strikes over salaries.

Many staff members openly talked about how much worse conditions had become because of inflation.

...

### What the Operating Room Taught Me About Responsibility

When someone enters an operating room, they place their life in your hands with complete trust.

One small mistake from us can leave a permanent impact on a person forever.

...

The patient should never feel that burden from you.

That realization changed the way I saw healthcare workers completely.

***

## Medical Supply Shortages in Iranian Hospitals

And at the same time, I watched the system itself slowly begin to break down.

Supplies became limited.

Things that used to be disposable started getting reused because hospitals could no longer afford enough replacements.

LMA airways.
Syringes.
Basic equipment.

...

### Drug Shortages and Patient Safety

Medications had to be used carefully because shortages became common.

I even remember periods where drugs like Propofol became difficult to find.

And that was terrifying.

...

***

## Why My View of Medicine Changed

Over time, something inside me slowly changed.

I still respected medicine.

I still respected healthcare workers deeply.

But I also started realizing that the version of the future I had imagined no longer matched reality.

...

***

## Lessons I Learned From Healthcare and Education

Even after everything, I don’t regret studying medicine.

Because the hospital gave me something education alone never could:

Perspective.

It taught me responsibility.
It taught me how fragile people are.
It taught me how much trust exists between strangers during the worst moments of their lives.

...

Some of the most hardworking, patient, and compassionate people I’ve ever met were healthcare workers trying to do their best under incredibly difficult conditions.

That experience changed me permanently.

***

## Conclusion

Maybe that’s why I no longer believe success is simply about following the path people hand to you.

Sometimes success is realizing when a system is too small for the future you want to build — and having the courage to search for another path before it slowly crushes your ambition.
